headline: Bobby Witt Jr. Goes First Overall to North Correa
subheadline: Bobby Witt Jr. went first overall, the Benchwarmers pulled off the draft's sneakiest heist, and somewhere in Albuquerque, a fantasy manager is already planning their victory parade — here's everything that happened when 14 obsessed managers logged on and started ruining their Sundays for the next six months.
body:
# 2026 MillerLite® BeerLeagueBaseball Draft Review
### *Presented without apology, sleep, or objectivity*

---

## **I. The War Room Opens**

You know that scene in *Moneyball* where Billy Beane is on the phone making trades while everyone else is just vibing, and you realize halfway through that some people in that room genuinely have no idea what's happening? That's every fantasy draft, every year, forever. Fourteen managers, one snake, two hundred and ten picks, and an unknowable number of bad decisions that won't reveal themselves until August. The 2026 MillerLite® BeerLeagueBaseball draft went down on March 22nd, and I am here to tell you it was *messy* in the best possible way — like a season two premiere of a show you forgot you loved. There were steals so blatant they should come with a ski mask. There were reaches so aggressive they require a formal apology. And there were a couple of managers who clearly did their homework, which in fantasy baseball terms means they either used a cheat sheet or they are actually unwell in a way that requires professional attention. (Both are valid. One wins championships.)

The big storylines walking in: **Sugar Land Skeeters** enter as defending champions, which means everyone drafted with a target on their back while the Skeeters drafted with the swagger of a team that knows the blueprint. **BaseballPro77** and **Abbotsford-Colby Twins** are the two-time championship clubs looking to add more hardware. **North Correa** and **General Hospital** are the bottom-dwellers trying to prove their losing streaks are a fluke and not a personality. And then there's **Benchwarmers**, who went 14th-7th-9th and appear to be stuck in the purgatory of fantasy baseball — never bad enough to get the good picks, never good enough to win anything. Until today. Because what Benchwarmers did at picks 14 and 15 might be the best two-pick sequence of the entire draft, and I am not being hyperbolic, which is something I only say when I *am* being hyperbolic, but this time I genuinely mean it.

Draft day for a league like this is basically the Super Bowl pregame show compressed into three hours. The trash talk in the chat, the last-minute injury panics, someone refreshing beat-reporter Twitter accounts like they're waiting for parole. The 2026 class had real juice at the top — a legitimate argument for six or seven different players as the first overall pick — and the draft board got genuinely interesting around picks 10-14, where late-lottery managers had to decide between upside and safety. Buckle in. This thing ran deep.

---

## **II. Draft Order & Early-Pick Breakdown**

**North Correa** held the first overall pick and made the call that, honestly, is almost impossible to argue: **Bobby Witt Jr.** to open the 2026 MillerLite® BeerLeagueBaseball draft. Coming off a 2025 season where Witt was arguably the best fantasy player in baseball — elite batting average, 30-plus homers, 30-plus steals, playing shortstop every day for a Royals team that's actually fun — this is the safest pick in the pool. The fact that North Correa has finished 14th and 12th the last two years makes this pick feel like the first episode of a redemption arc. (Whether the rest of the draft holds up is a different story, and we'll get to that.) From there, the board basically played chalk: **Athletic Park Dragons** got **Shohei Ohtani (Batter)** at two — yes, just the batting slash, but that batting slash produced a 171 wRC+ and 54 homers in 2024 and remains the most valuable non-pitching fantasy asset alive — and **Wisconsin Rapids Reapers** grabbed **Mookie Betts** third, which feels slightly high for a guy whose 2025 was injury-interrupted, but the multi-position eligibility (2B, SS, OF) makes Betts an absolute roster Swiss Army knife. **Abbotsford-Colby Twins** took **Aaron Judge** at four, **BaseballPro77** snagged **Elly De La Cruz** at five (high-upside pick, elite SB ceiling), **Albuquerque Bombers** locked in the ageless **José Ramírez** at six, and **Hiroshima Radiation** landed **Juan Soto** at seven. The back half of Round 1 was where it got fun: **Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs** took **Vlad Guerrero Jr.** at eight (a player coming off a massive power resurgence), **Buttville Skidmarks** grabbed **Kyle Tucker** at nine in what might be the best value in the first round given Tucker's 2025 numbers, and **General Hospital** — currently on a two-year last-place death march — took **Francisco Lindor** at ten. **Sugar Land Skeeters** grabbed **Fernando Tatis Jr.** at eleven (two-time champs running it back), **Riverside Roughnecks** took breakout candidate **Jackson Chourio** at twelve, **Canberra Wombats** went **Corbin Carroll** at thirteen, and **Benchwarmers** closed out Round 1 with **Julio Rodríguez** — and then immediately pivoted to the best pick in Round 2. More on that in a second.

---

## **III. The Draft Grades: All 14 Teams**

**North Correa** | *Pick #1*
Starting with **Bobby Witt Jr.** at first overall is correct, full stop. But then the wheels wobble a little. **Pete Alonso** in Round 2 is a fine bat, though the move to Baltimore raises questions. **Logan Gilbert** in Round 3 is a solid SP2 with good ratios. The problem is the back half — **Sandy Alcantara** and **Spencer Strider** in Rounds 8-9 are two of the highest-upside injury fliers in the entire draft, which is either genius or hubris, and with these two specifically it's probably 60/40 hubris. This roster has real talent but needs everything to break right from players who have not been healthy recently. For a team that finished 14th and 12th, this draft needed to be bulletproof, and it isn't quite. **Grade: B-** — *Witt is a foundation, but the injury-roulette pitching staff is a gamble North Correa can't afford.*

**Athletic Park Dragons** | *Pick #2*
**Shohei Ohtani (Batter)** at two is correct and glorious. But here's the thing — the Dragons finished *14th* last year after a 6th in 2024, which means something is structurally broken. The answer appears to be pitching, and they addressed it with aggression: **Michael King**, **Shota Imanaga**, **Aaron Nola**, and **Luis Castillo** in Rounds 4-7 is a starting rotation that would make a real GM jealous. **Matt Olson** in Round 2 gives them another power bat to park behind Ohtani. **Andrés Muñoz** in Round 8 is a filthy late-inning arm. The lineup is a little thin behind Olson, but the pitching infrastructure here is genuinely impressive. **Grade: A-** — *The pitching overhaul is real, and Ohtani plus Olson is a terrifying top of the order.*

**Wisconsin Rapids Reapers** | *Pick #3*
**Mookie Betts** at three is a slight reach — probably a pick-four or five player — but the position flexibility is worth one slot of premium. The Reapers then went heavily on depth, grabbing **Ketel Marte** in Round 2 (good value), **William Contreras** in Round 3, and **Matt McLain** in Round 4. The back end of this draft is almost entirely bullpen arms — **Justin Martinez**, **Jeff Hoffman**, **Jason Foley**, **Jason Adam** — which tells me the Reapers are deliberately targeting the NSVH category, which in a 12-cat league is smart asymmetric strategy. The lineup is okay but not scary. **Grade: B** — *Solid, smart, but lacks a true difference-maker after Mookie.*

**Abbotsford-Colby Twins** | *Pick #4*
The defending champs (2024 title, consistent top-10 finisher) come in with **Aaron Judge** at four, which is correct, and then immediately improve by grabbing **Jazz Chisholm Jr.** in Round 2 — multi-position eligibility (2B, 3B, OF), speed, power, and the kind of personality that wins you fantasy points and makes real GMs nervous. **Austin Riley** in Round 3 gives them a third masher. **Pablo López** in Round 4 is a quality SP anchor. The concern is that they got a little reach-y mid-draft — **Marcell Ozuna** to Pittsburgh feels like a weird fit — but overall this roster has the bones of a repeat contender. The Twins know how to draft. **Grade: A-** — *Judge-Chisholm-Riley is a three-headed fantasy monster, and they've got the pitching to match.*

**BaseballPro77** | *Pick #5*
Two-time champion, back-to-back top-two finishes before a slide to 7th. **Elly De La Cruz** at five is a legitimate first-round talent — the stolen base upside alone is a category winner — and then BaseballPro77 went and got **Jackson Merrill** in Round 2 (coming off a sneaky good 2025) and **Cole Ragans** in Round 3, who has SP1 stuff on a good day. **Emmanuel Clase** in Round 4 anchors the saves category — the guy's ERA and WHIP are basically fictional, in a good way. **Junior Caminero** in Round 7 is the dart throw that could pay off enormously by September. This team has championship DNA and drafted like it. **Grade: A** — *The two-time champ drafted with calm, calculated aggression and has the saves locked down early.*

**Albuquerque Bombers** | *Pick #6*
The Bombers finished third last year and are pushing to close that gap. **José Ramírez** at six is perfect value — he's a top-four pick every year and a three-category machine (HR/RBI/SB). **Freddie Freeman** in Round 2 adds another elite bat, **Garrett Crochet** in Round 3 is a strikeout-per-inning arm now with a full season on a contender, and **Logan Webb** in Round 5 gives them a QS machine. The middle rounds are solid if unspectacular. **Willy Adames** in Round 6 and **Xander Bogaerts** in Round 7 give them veteran stability. This team doesn't blow you away, but it's relentlessly consistent, which tracks with their trajectory. **Grade: B+** — *Ramírez-Freeman-Crochet is a legitimate top-three core, and the Bombers always maximize what they have.*

**Hiroshima Radiation** | *Pick #7*
**Juan Soto** at seven is absolutely correct — post-Yankee Soto in a Mets lineup with Pete Alonso is a fantasy goldmine. **Trea Turner** in Round 2 adds speed and average at shortstop. The pitching is where this gets interesting: **Blake Snell** in Round 3 (motivated, new contract, Dodger lineup), **Tyler Glasnow** in Round 5. Both are aces. Both have spent more time on the IL than your average MASH unit. **Jose Altuve** in Round 4 is a sneaky good pick — he quietly hit .307 in 2025. The Radiation have massive upside but are one injury cascade away from a top-five finish becoming an 11th. Classic boom-or-bust draft. **Grade: B+** — *Soto-Turner-Snell is a headline grab, but the injury exposure in the rotation is real and significant.*

**Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs** | *Pick #8*
The Wonderdogs have been to the fantasy playoffs every year — 4th, 5th, 2nd — and they draft accordingly: no gambling, just quality. **Vlad Guerrero Jr.** at eight is tremendous value for a guy who posted 40 homers in 2025. **Bryce Harper** in Round 2 gives them two elite 1B options (Harper has OF eligibility in some formats). **Corbin Burnes** in Round 3 and **Framber Valdez** in Round 4 — two SP1-caliber arms who eat innings. **Ronald Acuña Jr.** in Round 5 is the highest-upside pick of the middle rounds, a guy returning from injury who, when healthy, is literally the best fantasy player alive. **Devin Williams** in Round 7 locks down the saves column. This team is built to be in it every week. **Grade: A** — *The most balanced roster in the draft — scary batting, elite pitching, and Acuña as a boom card off the bench.*

**Buttville Skidmarks** | *Pick #9*
**Kyle Tucker** at nine might legitimately be the best value in Round 1. Tucker's 2025 numbers (29 HR, .286 AVG, strong OBP) make him a top-six pick, and getting him at nine is just straight-up stealing. **Zack Wheeler** in Round 2 is one of the five best fantasy pitchers in baseball. **Rafael Devers** in Round 3 is a pure mash machine. **Corey Seager** in Round 4 — look, Seager's injury history is real, but when he's right he's a .300-30-HR shortstop. **Jacob deGrom** in Round 5 is the biggest gamble in the draft — if he's healthy he's an SP1, if he's not you have a very expensive DL slot. The Skidmarks have been middle-of-the-pack (11th, 8th, 8th) but this roster has legitimate contender upside. **Grade: B+** — *Tucker-Wheeler is an absurd 1-2 punch and the deGrom dice roll could be the swing pick of the entire season.*

**General Hospital** | *Pick #10*
Listen. Two straight last-place finishes (13th, 13th). And then they draft **Francisco Lindor** at ten — which is fine, Lindor is a premium fantasy SS — but the supporting cast is concerning. **Jarren Duran** in Round 2 (a real breakout pick, actually), **Chris Sale** in Round 3 (ERA, WHIP, K upside is real IF he's healthy), and then... **Mike Trout** in Round 6? Trout hasn't played a full season since 2019. This is a sentimental pick dressed up as a strategy pick. The General Hospital experience continues. The mid-rounds lack firepower, and the pitching is mostly a collection of question marks. **Grade: C** — *Lindor and Duran are real, but Trout-Sale injury dependence is a hospital waiting room, which, given the team name, feels almost too on-brand.*

**Sugar Land Skeeters** | *Pick #11*
Defending champions. Last three finishes: 13th, 3rd, 1st. The Skeeters have the best glow-up narrative in the league. **Fernando Tatis Jr.** at eleven is solid value — 33 HR, 29 SB type of season when locked in. **Tarik Skubal** in Round 2 is the steal of the second round — he was *the* best pitcher in baseball in 2025 and somehow slid to pick 18. **Cody Bellinger** in Round 3 has OF/1B flexibility and bounce-back potential. **Yoshinobu Yamamoto** in Round 4, if healthy, is a top-10 SP. **Ryan Helsley** in Round 7 is an elite closer. The Skeeters know what they're doing and they drafted like champions. **Grade: A** — *Skubal at 18 might be the single best value pick in the draft, and the defending champs look ready to run it back.*

**Riverside Roughnecks** | *Pick #12*
**Jackson Chourio** at twelve is a fascinating pick — he's 21, he posted 25 HR and 40+ SB upside in 2025, and in a categories league the stolen bases alone make him top-10 material. **Gunnar Henderson** in Round 2 is a bargain — 30+ HR, elite SS, only getting better. **Ozzie Albies** in Round 3, **Manny Machado** in Round 4. This lineup is stacked. **Mason Miller** in Round 6 is one of the best closers in baseball. **Roki Sasaki** in Round 7 — look, if he puts it together in LA with that rotation behind him, this is a top-5 SP pick. The Roughnecks have never finished higher than 9th but this roster has the talent to end that. **Grade: A-** — *Chourio-Henderson-Miller is the best value trio in the draft, and the Roughnecks finally have the pieces to escape mediocrity.*

**Canberra Wombats** | *Pick #13*
The Wombats have an odd-year pattern going (3rd in 2023, 4th in 2025) and this draft fits that profile. **Corbin Carroll** at thirteen is decent value — speed, average, OF flexibility. But then they pivoted hard with **Yordan Alvarez** in Round 2, which is an absolutely elite pick that late in the draft. **Wyatt Langford** in Round 3 is a high-ceiling breakout candidate. **Josh Hader** in Round 5 locks down saves. **Cal Raleigh** in Round 6 is the best catching value in the draft — 30+ HR from the catcher position is absurd. **Félix Bautista** in Round 9, if healthy coming back from Tommy John, is a lottery ticket worth scratching. This team is quietly loaded. **Grade: A-** — *Alvarez in Round 2 might be the biggest steal of the draft, and the Wombats look ready to crash someone's party in October.*

**Benchwarmers** | *Pick #14*
Here's where I lose my mind a little. The Benchwarmers pick 14th and 15th in a snake. They take **Julio Rodríguez** at 14 — fine, love the upside — and then immediately grab **Paul Skenes** at 15. Paul. Skenes. The best pitching prospect-turned-ace in baseball, coming off a rookie campaign that was historically good by strikeout and FIP metrics, and he's *still available* at pick 15? The Benchwarmers then pivoted to **Oneil Cruz** in Round 3 (one of the most electric athletes in baseball), **Dylan Cease** in Round 4 (strikeout machine), and **Edwin Díaz** in Round 5 (elite closer). **Adley Rutschman** in Round 6 is one of the three best catchers in fantasy. This team went 14-7-9, never quite breaking through, and yet this might be their best draft ever. **Grade: A+** — *The Skenes pick alone would justify an A, and the Benchwarmers stacked their roster top to bottom in a way that should make every other manager in this league nervous.*

---

## **IV. Draft Day Heroes & Villains**

**Three Best Value Picks:**

🏆 **Paul Skenes, SP, Pittsburgh Pirates — Benchwarmers, Round 2, Pick 15.** The consensus ace of his generation, available at the 15th pick of the entire draft. If this were the stock market, the SEC would investigate. Skenes' 2025 ERA and K-rate were generational, and the Benchwarmers got him like they were shopping a clearance rack. Unreal.

🥈 **Tarik Skubal, SP, Detroit Tigers — Sugar Land Skeeters, Round 2, Pick 18.** The 2025 AL Cy Young winner going 18th overall is the kind of thing that makes you check if the draft software glitched. The defending champs added the best pitcher in baseball in Round 2. That's just mean.

🥉 **Yordan Alvarez, OF, Houston Astros — Canberra Wombats, Round 2, Pick 16.** A .300-30-HR-1.000 OPS machine available in the middle of the second round. In a snake draft, Alvarez at 16 is the equivalent of finding a parking spot right in front of the restaurant on a Saturday night. Everything aligned.

**Three Worst Reaches:**

💀 **Mike Trout, OF, Los Angeles Angels — General Hospital, Round 6, Pick 75.** I respect the mythology. I do not respect drafting a guy who has played fewer than 50 games in multiple recent seasons as your Round 6 anchor when your team already has two straight last-place finishes. This is the fantasy equivalent of buying a house because you loved the curb appeal and ignoring that the foundation is crumbling.

💀 **Corbin Carroll, OF, Arizona Diamondbacks — Canberra Wombats, Round 1, Pick 13.** Carroll's 2025 was a step back from his breakout, and drafting him in the back of Round 1 when the Wombats then grabbed Yordan Alvarez two picks later suggests the draft board priority might have been slightly scrambled. Carroll is a Round 2-3 player right now, not a Round 1 lock.

💀 **Jacob deGrom, SP, Texas Rangers — Buttville Skidmarks, Round 5, Pick 65.** The most talented pitcher alive who currently seems to exist only in theory. Round 5 for a guy whose last healthy full season was 2021 is aggressive optimism bordering on medical denial.

---

## **V. Five Bold Predictions for 2026**

1. **The Benchwarmers win the championship.** I know. I said it. The Skenes-Rodríguez-Cease core is elite, Rutschman steadies the catcher slot, and the Benchwarmers finally drafted a complete team instead of an interesting one. Sometimes it's just your year, and this draft says 2026 is theirs.

2. **General Hospital finishes 13th or 14th for the third straight year.** I want to be wrong. The **Mike Trout** pick alone tells me something is philosophically broken in that war room, and **Lindor** can't carry a roster with this many injury dependencies.

3. **The Riverside Roughnecks make their first-ever top-five finish.** The **Chourio-Henderson** combo at 12 and 17 is the best back-to-back value sequence at the position in the draft. **Mason Miller** locks down saves. If **Roki Sasaki** figures it out in LA, this team is genuinely scary.

4. **Spencer Strider, drafted by North Correa in Round 9, becomes the most important player on any roster in the back half of the season.** If he returns from elbow surgery healthy and dominant, **North Correa** could go from 12th to playoff contention in a season. Big if. Enormous if. But the upside is real.

5. **The Albuquerque Bombers finally break through and win their first title.** They've been knocking — 5th, 10th, 3rd — and the **Ramírez-Freeman-Crochet** core is as clean as any three-slot in the league. The Bombers have been one piece away, and this year's draft gave them enough depth to stay healthy deep into the season.

---

## **VI. The Pick: Champion Crowned**

My 2026 MillerLite® BeerLeagueBaseball champion is the **Benchwarmers**, and I'm not hedging.

Here's the case: **Julio Rodríguez** gives you speed, average, and power. **Paul Skenes** — again, the best pitcher in baseball, available at pick 15, I will not stop being amazed by this — anchors a rotation that also includes **Dylan Cease** (elite swing-and-miss stuff, Toronto rotation is respectable) and **Tanner Bibee** in Round 8 (quietly one of the most efficient starters in the AL). **Adley Rutschman** is the best two-way catcher in baseball. **Edwin Díaz** and **Tanner Scott** give them saves and holds. **Oneil Cruz** in Round 3 is the kind of pick that ends up on a "how did we let this happen" list at year's end.

This team finished 14th, then 7th, then 9th. That's not mediocrity — that's a team that keeps getting tantalizingly close to figuring it out. And in 2026, they drafted like a team that finally had the answer key. The Skenes pick is the moment this franchise changes. Championship windows open and close faster than a bad Netflix show, and the Benchwarmers just kicked theirs wide open.

In the immortal wisdom of the great philosopher Kevin Garnett: *anything is possible.* But some things are more possible than others, and a rotation headlined by Paul Skenes, with Julio Rodríguez stealing bags and Adley Rutschman quietly putting up a .380 OBP, feels less like a fantasy dream and more like an inevitability in progress.

Benchwarmers. Write it down. Pour the MillerLite® early.

---

*The 2026 MillerLite® BeerLeagueBaseball season begins now. Good luck. You'll need it. Well — most of you will need it.*
